questions.text: When alcohols react with Na metal, the alkoxide ion thus formed is a strong:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: When alcohols react with sodium metal (Na), they undergo a metalation reaction to form alkoxide ions. The reaction can be represented as follows, using methanol (CH3OH) as an example: CH3OH + Na → CH3ONa + 1/2 H2↑ Here, the hydrogen gas ( 1/2 H2 ) is evolved as a byproduct. The product, CH3ONa, is the alkoxide ion. Alkoxide ions are strong nucleophiles because they carry a negative charge on the oxygen atom. This negative charge makes the oxygen atom electron-rich and thus highly reactive toward electrophiles in chemical reactions.

questions.text: An organic compound X is prepared by the oxidation of ethanol. This X reacts with ethanol to produce an ester, what is X likely to be:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The organic compound X, which is prepared by the oxidation of ethanol, is likely to be ethanoic acid, also known as acetic acid. The oxidation of ethanol can be represented as follows: CH3CH2OH → CH3COOH Here, ethanol ( CH3CH2OH ) undergoes oxidation to form ethanoic acid ( CH3COOH ), also known as acetic acid. Ethanoic acid is a carboxylic acid and can react with ethanol to produce an ester through an esterification reaction. The general esterification reaction is as follows: Carboxylic acid + Alcohol → Ester + Water In the case of ethanoic acid and ethanol, the esterification reaction can be represented as: CH3COOH + CH3CH2OH → CH3COOCH2CH3 + H2O The product is ethyl acetate ( CH3COOCH2CH3), which is an ester.

In the presence of a mild oxidizing agent, such as potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) in acidic solution, primary alcohols are oxidized to aldehydes. For example, the oxidation of ethanol (CH3CH2OH) gives acetaldehyde (CH3CHO). If the reaction is carried out in the presence of a more powerful oxidizing agent, such as potassium permanganate (KMnO4), the aldehyde is further oxidized to a carboxylic acid. For example, the oxidation of acetaldehyde gives acetic acid (CH3COOH). Here is the balanced chemical equation for the oxidation of ethanol to acetaldehyde: CH3CH2OH + K2Cr2O7 + 4H2SO4 → CH3CHO + K2SO4 + 4H2O + Cr2(SO4)3 And here is the balanced chemical equation for the oxidation of acetaldehyde to acetic acid: CH3CHO + 2KMnO4 + 3H2SO4 → CH3COOH + 2K2SO4 + 3H2O + 2MnSO4

The protonated alcohol then undergoes a nucleophilic attack by the oxidizing agent. The oxidizing agent donates a pair of electrons to the carbonyl carbon of the alcohol, forming a new bond between the oxidizing agent and the alcohol. The carbonyl group is then hydrolyzed, resulting in the formation of an aldehyde. 2.The second step of the oxidation of secondary alcohols is also catalyzed by a strong oxidizing agent. In this step, the aldehyde is oxidized to a ketone. The hydrogen peroxide donates a pair of electrons to the carbonyl carbon of the aldehyde, forming a new bond between the hydrogen peroxide and the aldehyde. The carbocation is stabilized by the interaction of its positive charge with the electrons of the neighboring carbon atoms. The carbocation loses a proton to form an alkene.
